concepts.title: Amines are classified into three types based on the alkyl group attached to the nitrogen atom.
concepts.description: Primary amines are those in which the alkyl group is attached to the nitrogen atom directly. Secoindary amines have two alkyl groups attached to the nitrogen atom. Tertiary amines have three alkyl groups attached to the nitrogen atom.

concepts.title: Preparation of amines
concepts.description: Amines can be prepared by the reaction of ammonia with alkyl halides, by reduction of nitriles and nitro compounds, and by the reaction of ammonia with alcohols.

concepts.title: Physical properties of amines
concepts.description: Amines are weak bases and have a characteristic fishy smell. They are generally soluble in water and organic solvents. Their boiling points are higher than those of corresponding hydrocarbons.

concepts.title: Acid-base properties of amines
concepts.description: Amines are basic in nature. They can accept a proton and form a conjugate acid. To remember this, use the mnemonic: 'Am I Not Basic?'

concepts.title: Reaction of amines with acid chlorides
concepts.description: Students often mistake the reaction of amines with acid chlorides to form amides. However, the correct product is an imide. To avoid this mistake, remember that amines react with acid chlorides to form compounds with one less oxygen atom.

concepts.title: pKa of amines
concepts.description: The pKa values of amines are in the range of 9-10. This indicates that they are weak bases.

concepts.title: Uses of amines
concepts.description: Amines have various uses, including the manufacture of dyes, pharmaceuticals, and surfactants.

concepts.title: Preparation of alkyl halides from amines
concepts.description: Amines can be converted to alkyl halides by reacting them with hydrogen halides, such as HCl or HBr.
